turns.content_text:
We're currently solving the following issue within our repository. Here's the issue text:
ISSUE:
IndexError with pytest's fixtures.py
```
% pyupgrade --py3-only --keep-percent-format src/_pytest/fixtures.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "…/Vcs/pytest/.venv/bin/pyupgrade", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "…/Vcs/pytest/.venv/lib/python3.7/site-packages/pyupgrade.py", line 1428, in main
    ret |= fix_file(filename, args)
  File "…/Vcs/pytest/.venv/lib/python3.7/site-packages/pyupgrade.py", line 1402, in fix_file
    contents_text = _fix_py3_plus(contents_text)
  File "…/Vcs/pytest/.venv/lib/python3.7/site-packages/pyupgrade.py", line 1244, in _fix_py3_plus
    _replace_call(tokens, i, end, func_args, template)
  File "…/Vcs/pytest/.venv/lib/python3.7/site-packages/pyupgrade.py", line 1171, in _replace_call
    src = tmpl.format(args=arg_strs, rest=rest)
IndexError: list index out of range
```
`print(repr(tmpl), repr(arg_strs), repr(rest))` shows:

> 'raise {args[1]}.with_traceback({args[2]})' ['*err'] ''

INSTRUCTIONS:
Now, you're going to solve this issue on your own. Your terminal session has started and you're in the repository's root directory. You can use any bash commands or the special interface to help you. Edit all the files you need to and run any checks or tests that you want.
Remember, YOU CAN ONLY ENTER ONE COMMAND AT A TIME. You should always wait for feedback after every command.
When you're satisfied with all of the changes you've made, you can submit your changes to the code base by simply running the submit command.
Note however that you cannot use any interactive session commands (e.g. python, vim) in this environment, but you can write scripts and run them. E.g. you can write a python script and then run it with `python <script_name>.py`.

NOTE ABOUT THE EDIT COMMAND: Indentation really matters! When editing a file, make sure to insert appropriate indentation before each line!

IMPORTANT TIPS:
1. Always start by trying to replicate the bug that the issues discusses.
   If the issue includes code for reproducing the bug, we recommend that you re-implement that in your environment, and run it to make sure you can reproduce the bug.
   Then start trying to fix it.
   When you think you've fixed the bug, re-run the bug reproduction script to make sure that the bug has indeed been fixed.

   If the bug reproduction script does not print anything when it successfully runs, we recommend adding a print("Script completed successfully, no errors.") command at the end of the file,
   so that you can be sure that the script indeed ran fine all the way through.

2. If you run a command and it doesn't work, try running a different command. A command that did not work once will not work the second time unless you modify it!

3. If you open a file and need to get to an area around a specific line that is not in the first 100 lines, say line 583, don't just use the scroll_down command multiple times. Instead, use the goto 583 command. It's much quicker.

4. If the bug reproduction script requires inputting/reading a specific file, such as buggy-input.png, and you'd like to understand how to input that file, conduct a search in the existing repo code, to see whether someone else has already done that. Do this by running the command: find_file "buggy-input.png" If that doesn't work, use the linux 'find' command.

5. Always make sure to look at the currently open file and the current working directory (which appears right after the currently open file). The currently open file might be in a different directory than the working directory! Note that some commands, such as 'create', open files, so they might change the current  open file.

6. When editing files, it is easy to accidentally specify a wrong line number or to write code with incorrect indentation. Always check the code after you issue an edit to make sure that it reflects what you wanted to accomplish. If it didn't, issue a

To start solving this issue, we need to reproduce the error first. The issue provides a command to reproduce the error. Let's run the `pyupgrade --py3-only --keep-percent-format src/_pytest/fixtures.py` command to see if we encounter the same error.
```
pyupgrade --py3-only --keep-percent-format src/_pytest/fixtures.py
```

1:# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
2:from __future__ import absolute_import
3:from __future__ import unicode_literals
4:
5:import ast
6:import sys
7:
8:import pytest
9:
10:from pyupgrade import _fix_format_literals
11:from pyupgrade import _fix_fstrings
12:from pyupgrade import _fix_percent_format
13:from pyupgrade import _fix_py2_compatible
14:from pyupgrade import _fix_py3_plus
15:from pyupgrade import _fix_tokens
16:from pyupgrade import _imports_unicode_literals
17:from pyupgrade import _is_bytestring
18:from pyupgrade import _percent_to_format
19:from pyupgrade import _simplify_conversion_flag
20:from pyupgrade import main
21:from pyupgrade import parse_format
22:from pyupgrade import parse_percent_format
23:from pyupgrade import unparse_parsed_string
24:
25:
26:@pytest.mark.parametrize(
27:    's',
28:    (
29:        '', 'foo', '{}', '{0}', '{named}', '{!r}', '{:>5}', '{{', '}}',
30:        '{0!s:15}'
31:    ),
32:)
33:def test_roundtrip_text(s):
34:    assert unparse_parsed_string(parse_format(s)) == s
35:
36:
37:@pytest.mark.parametrize(
38:    ('s', 'expected'),
39:    (
40:        ('{:}', '{}'),
41:        ('{0:}', '{0}'),
42:        ('{0!r:}', '{0!r}'),
43:    ),
44:)
45:def test_intentionally_not_round_trip(s, expected):
46:    # Our unparse simplifies empty parts, whereas stdlib allows them
47:    ret = unparse_parsed_string(parse_format(s))
48:    assert ret == expected
49:
50:
51:@pytest.mark.parametrize(
52:    ('s', 'expected'),
53:    (
54:        # Don't touch empty set literals
55:        ('set()', 'set()'),
56:        # Don't touch set(empty literal) with newlines in them (may create
57:        # syntax errors)
58:        ('set((\n))', 'set((\n))'),
59:        # Don't touch weird looking function calls -- use autopep8 or such
60:        # first
61:        ('set (())', 'set (())'),
62:        ('set ((1, 2))', 'set ((1, 2))'),
63:        # Take a set literal with an empty tuple / list and remove the arg
64:        ('set(())', 'set()'),
65:        ('set([])', 'set()'),
66:        # Remove spaces in empty set literals
67:        ('set(( ))', 'set()'),
68:        # Some "normal" test cases
69:        ('set((1, 2))', '{1, 2}'),
70:        ('set([1, 2])', '{1, 2}'),
71:        ('set(x for x in y)', '{x for x in y}'),
72:        ('set([x for x in y])', '{x for x in y}'),
73:        # These are strange cases -- the ast doesn't tell us about the parens
74:        # here so we have to parse ourselves
75:        ('set((x for x in y))', '{x for x in y}'),
76:        ('set(((1, 2)))', '{1, 2}'),
77:        # The ast also doesn't tell us about the start of the tuple in this
78:        # generator expression
79:        ('set((a, b) for a, b in y)', '{(a, b) for a, b in y}'),
80:        # The ast also doesn't tell us about the start of the tuple for
81:        # tuple of tuples
82:        ('set(((1, 2), (3, 4)))', '{(1, 2), (3, 4)}'),
83:        # Lists where the first element is a tuple also gives the ast trouble
84:        # The first element lies about the offset of the element
85:        ('set([(1, 2), (3, 4)])', '{(1, 2), (3, 4)}'),
86:        (
87:            'set(\n'
88:            '    [(1, 2)]\n'
89:            ')',
90:            '{\n'
91:            '    (1, 2)\n'
92:            '}',
93:        ),
94:        ('set([((1, 2)), (3, 4)])', '{((1, 2)), (3, 4)}'),
95:        # And it gets worse
96:        ('set((((1, 2),),))', '{((1, 2),)}'),
97:        # Some multiline cases
98:        ('set(\n(1, 2))', '{\n1, 2}'),
99:        ('set((\n1,\n2,\n))\n', '{\n1,\n2,\n}\n'),
100:        # Nested sets
